markdown.markdown:
Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started! - Be efficient – Get stuff done quickly - Be effective – Use the right skills for the right tasks Skills - Create new Excel workbooks and work on multiple worksheets - Insert data in Excel and perform basic calculations - Format cells according to their contents - Use absolute, relative, and mixed referencing - Use conditional formatting - Find and replace content in your worksheet ## Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems. **Insights** - It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic. 1. Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities. 2. Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes. - A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. [Mac](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/keyboard-shortcuts-in-excel-1798d9d5-842a-42b8-9c99-9b7213f0040f#picktab=macos) [Win](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/keyboard-shortcuts-in-excel-1798d9d5-842a-42b8-9c99-9b7213f0040f?ui=en-US&rs=en-US&ad=US) - There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists! - Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a 'variable' is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3). ## Be Effective - Get stuff done right In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader's opinion of your content (and of your abilities). **Insights** - Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know. - Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments. - You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for 'Data Storytelling' and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories. ## Exercise Overview **Objective** In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario. **Scenario** The idea came to you last semester. As you were cramming hard for your finals, the though hit you – Pizza Delivery by Drones, that could be a real business! You don’t know how it came to you. Maybe it’s because you had a passion for airplanes since you were a kid. Maybe it’s because you have owned all of the DJI quadcopters since the Phantom 1. Or maybe just because you were very hungry and had very little time to spare during finals week. You even have a name in mind for your firm: Papa Drones. Over the holiday break you saw your older cousing Anna, who is a partner at a large San Francisco based venture capital firm. You told her you wanted to ask your Information Systems professor for some help, but she told you that no serious person would listen to you before you did some preliminary analysis. Anna was intrigued by the idea and was willing to help you a bit in her spare time. She said: *“if you are serious about this idea Microsoft Excel is the instrument of choice to do the analysis.”* *“Of course!” you responded.* In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ```{=html} ``` ```{=html}
{{< include footer.html >}}
```
